babyMilestones.name: Baby Milestones
babyMilestones.description: What developmental milestones to expect by age. Overview based on the CDC framework.

meta.title: Baby Milestones Calculator — Developmental Steps 0–24 Months | Health Calculators
meta.description: What developmental milestones should your baby reach at each age? Overview for 2, 4, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18 and 24 months — based on the CDC framework. Free & instant.

babyMilestones.title: Baby Milestones
babyMilestones.description: What milestones should your baby reach at each age? Pick an age or enter a birth date and get an overview by CDC framework — motor, language, social, cognitive.
babyMilestones.introNote: This overview is not a substitute for routine well-child visits. Every child develops at their own pace — these milestones are markers, not pass/fail tests.
babyMilestones.warningsTitle: Watch out — when to call the pediatrician
babyMilestones.warningsIntro: Talk to your pediatrician if at this age your baby:
babyMilestones.expectedSizeNote: WHO median for healthy babies (averaged across sexes). Variation is normal — the growth curve in your child's chart is more meaningful than a single point.

milestones.liftHeadProne: Lifts head briefly when on tummy
milestones.noEyeFocus: can't focus eyes or follow faces
milestones.pushesUpProne: Pushes up on forearms when on tummy
milestones.showsBoredom: Shows boredom (cries) when activity stays the same
milestones.doesNotPushUp: doesn't push up when on tummy
milestones.transfersObjects: Passes objects from one hand to the other
milestones.stringsVowels: Strings vowels together when babbling (aaaah, ohhh)
milestones.exploresMouth: Explores things by putting them in mouth
milestones.curiosityForObjects: Shows curiosity — tries to get out-of-reach things
milestones.pullsToStand: Pulls up to stand on furniture
milestones.mayTakeSteps: May take a few steps unaided
milestones.doesNotCrawl: doesn't crawl or drags one side
milestones.pointsToShow: Points with finger to show things
milestones.doesNotPoint: doesn't point at things to show interest
milestones.usesSpoonCup: Eats with a spoon, drinks from a cup
milestones.doesNotNoticeCaregivers: doesn't notice when caregivers come or go
milestones.pointsToBodyParts: Points to body parts when named

babyMilestones.howItWorksText: This overview is based on the CDC's revised 2022 milestones (Learn the Signs. Act Early.) and the AAP/Bright Futures developmental surveillance recommendations. The eight reference ages (2, 4, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 24 months) align with US well-child visit checkpoints. For an age between two checkpoints, the nearest lower one is shown. Use corrected age for premature babies.
babyMilestones.disclaimer: Important: every child develops differently. This list does not replace well-child visits or pediatric assessment. If your baby loses skills they once had (skills loss) or several milestones are clearly missed, talk to your pediatrician.

faq.a: Milestones are typical developmental skills most children reach by a certain age. The CDC, for example, lists 'sits without support' as a 9-month milestone because ≥ 75 % of children reach it by then.

faq.q: My baby hasn't reached a milestone yet — is that bad?
faq.a: Usually not. Babies develop in spurts and at their own pace. A single delayed milestone is rarely a problem, but several missed milestones or a loss of skills already gained should be discussed with the pediatrician.

faq.q: Should I correct age for premature babies?
faq.a: Yes. For premature babies (born before 37 weeks of gestation), use corrected age — i.e. age from the original due date instead of the actual birth. The AAP recommends correction up to 2 years of age.

faq.q: What sources are these milestones based on?
faq.a: The list follows the CDC's revised 2022 overview (Learn the Signs. Act Early.) and AAP Bright Futures recommendations. The CDC partnered with the AAP and specialists in pediatrics and developmental assessment to produce the revision.

faq.q: When should I see the pediatrician?
faq.a: If your baby loses skills, doesn't react to sounds, doesn't make eye contact, doesn't smile or babble, or if your gut tells you something is off. The simplest rule is: when in doubt, ask.

faq.q: What is the right tracking cadence?
faq.a: Well-child visits at 2, 4, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 24 months align with this framework. They are the standard touchpoint and most insurance plans cover them fully. Between visits, occasional informal tracking is enough — there's no need to test every day.

You can support development by talking, reading, floor playtime and safe spaces to explore. Speeding it up beyond a child's biological pace is rarely possible — development follows a genetically guided plan.
